prompt: A probe passes the Moon at radius r = 4,087 km moving at v = 1.782 km/s — faster than escape speed, so its orbit is hyperbolic. With μ = 4.905×10^12 m³/s², the speed it retains infinitely far away is v_∞ = √(v² − 2μ/r). Compute v_∞ in km/s. Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.

A probe flies in a circular orbit 910 km above the surface of the Moon (radius R = 1.737×10^6 m, μ = 4.905×10^12 m³/s²). How fast is it travelling, in km/s? Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.
A probe flies in a circular orbit 830 km above the surface of Mars (radius R = 3.390×10^6 m, μ = 4.283×10^13 m³/s²). How fast is it travelling, in km/s? Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.

A spacecraft must move between two coplanar circular orbits about Jupiter, from radius r₁ = 7,400 km to r₂ = 37,200 km, using the most fuel-efficient two-impulse manoeuvre. The body's gravitational parameter is μ = 1.267×10^17 m³/s². What is the total speed change required, in m/s? Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.
At a distance r = 1,737 km from the center of the Moon (μ = 4.905×10^12 m³/s²), what is the minimum speed needed to escape its gravity, in km/s? Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.

A satellite in a 9,500 km circular Earth orbit is to be raised to a 28,100 km circular orbit with the least total propellant. Earth's μ = 3.986×10^14 m³/s². Report the total Δv (sum of both burns) in m/s. Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.
A satellite is held in a circular orbit of radius 9,100 km around the Moon (μ = 4.905×10^12 m³/s²). What is its orbital speed, in km/s? Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.

A satellite in a 7,600 km circular Earth orbit is to be raised to a 27,200 km circular orbit with the least total propellant. Earth's μ = 3.986×10^14 m³/s². Report the total Δv (sum of both burns) in m/s. Give your final answer as \boxed{value unit}.
